slides.title: Welcome to the Language Safari!
slides.text: Hello, Explorer! Welcome to English Week 1! This week, we are going on an exciting language safari. We will explore our neighborhood to spot word patterns. We will learn about words that end in double consonants. We will also learn how to point to things using pronouns. Finally, we will read stories about local adventures. Let's keep our eyes open and observe closely. Are you ready to start this learning adventure? For instance, consider the example of Weekly Goal: Master CVCC words, point to objects using pronouns, and explore story elements! To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Weekly Goal
examples.content: Master CVCC words, point to objects using pronouns, and explore story elements!

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Spotting Ending Sounds (CVCC)
slides.text: Look at your hand. Let's say it slowly: h-a-n-d. Hear how the letters 'n' and 'd' blend at the end? This is a CVCC word: Consonant-Vowel-Consonant-Consonant. It ends with two consonants that make a blended sound. We call these ending consonant blends in phonics. They are very common in the words we use every day. Let's learn how to spot and spell them correctly. We will search for CVCC words in our environment today! For instance, consider the example of Word Pattern: C = Consonant, V = Vowel, C = Consonant, C = Consonant. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Word Pattern
examples.content: C = Consonant, V = Vowel, C = Consonant, C = Consonant.

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Phonics Hunt: In the Tent
slides.text: Imagine we are camping under the dark night sky. We set up a strong green tent on the ground. Look at the word 'tent'. It ends with 'nt'. The letters 'n' and 't' blend together smoothly. Let's practice saying the word 'tent' three times. Feel the blend of 'n' and 't' on your tongue. We spell it as t - e - n - t. It is a perfect example of a CVCC word! To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Spelling
examples.content: t - e - n - t

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Phonics Hunt: Under the Lamp
slides.text: When the sun sets, the room becomes very dark. We turn on the brass lamp sitting on our desk. Look at the word 'lamp'. It ends with 'mp'. The letters 'm' and 'p' blend at the very end. What does a lamp help us observe at night? It shines a bright light on our notebook pages. We spell it as l - a - m - p. Can you hear the 'mp' blend at the end? To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Spelling
examples.content: l - a - m - p

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Phonics Hunt: The Pink Flower
slides.text: Let's take a walk in the green garden. We observe a beautiful pink flower opening its petals. Look at the word 'pink'. It ends with 'nk'. The letters 'n' and 'k' form a blended sound. How many pink things can you see in your room? Maybe a pink toy, a pink pen, or a pink shirt. We spell it as p - i - n - k. The ending blend is 'nk'. Say it: pink! To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Spelling
examples.content: p - i - n - k

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Phonics Hunt: Keeping Active
slides.text: To keep our bodies strong, we must stay active. We bend our knees before we run in the park. We wear a tight leather belt to hold our shorts. Look at 'bend' (ends in 'nd') and 'belt' (ends in 'lt'). Both of these words are CVCC spelling patterns. They show how consonants pair up at the end. We spell them as b-e-n-d and b-e-l-t. Practice writing these action words on your paper! For instance, consider the example of Spelling: b - e - n - d and b - e - l - t To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Spelling
examples.content: b - e - n - d  and  b - e - l - t

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Phonics Hunt: In the Wind
slides.text: Listen to the wind blowing through the bamboo trees. The word 'wind' ends with the blend 'nd'. After running in the wind, we feel very thirsty. We drink a cold glass of milk to get energy. The word 'milk' ends with the blend 'lk'. Both 'wind' and 'milk' are common CVCC words. We spell them as w-i-n-d and m-i-l-k. Read these words aloud to hear their ending sounds! For instance, consider the example of Spelling: w - i - n - d and m - i - l - k To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Spelling
examples.content: w - i - n - d  and  m - i - l - k

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Phonics Hunt: Active Play
slides.text: We sit at our wooden desk to write observations. The word 'desk' ends with the blend 'sk'. We go on an insect hunt in the garden grass. The word 'hunt' ends with the blend 'nt'. Both 'desk' and 'hunt' help us talk about play. They follow the CVCC consonant blending pattern. We spell them as d-e-s-k and h-u-n-t. Can you spell them without looking at the screen? For instance, consider the example of Spelling: d - e - s - k and h - u - n - t To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Spelling
examples.content: d - e - s - k  and  h - u - n - t

slides.title: Grammar: Pointing to Things
slides.text: When we observe nature, we need to point to things. We use special words called demonstrative pronouns. These pronouns tell us if an object is near or far. They also tell us if there is one object or many. The four pronouns are: this, that, these, and those. They help make our descriptions clear and specific. Let's learn how to use them like real scientists. We will practice pointing to objects in our room! For instance, consider the example of Focus Pronouns: This, That, These, Those To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Focus Pronouns
examples.content: This, That, These, Those

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Near Us: This and These
slides.text: Look at the pencil you are holding in your hand. You can say: 'This is my pencil.' (one object). Now look at the fingers on your own hand. You can say: 'These are my fingers.' (many objects). We use 'this' for a single object near us. We use 'these' for multiple objects close to us. Both words show that the objects are within reach. Try touching a book and saying: 'This is my book.' For instance, consider the example of Near Examples: This pencil (1 near), These rocks (many near). To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Near Examples
examples.content: This pencil (1 near), These rocks (many near).

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Far From Us: That and Those
slides.text: Look out the window at the distant sky. You see a bird and say: 'That is a bird.' (one far). You see clouds and say: 'Those are clouds.' (many far). We use 'that' for a single object far away. We use 'those' for multiple objects far away. These words show that the objects are out of reach. Point to a distant tree and say: 'That is a tree.' Point to stars and say: 'Those are stars.' For instance, consider the example of Far Examples: That star (1 far), Those mountains (many far). To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Far Examples
examples.content: That star (1 far), Those mountains (many far).

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Sentence Engine: Declarative Sentences
slides.text: A telling sentence is like a small train. It makes a statement to tell a fact or observation. We call this a declarative sentence in grammar. It must always start with a capital letter. It must always end with a period punctuation mark. It has a subject (who or what the sentence is about). It has a predicate (what the subject is or does). Let's build a declarative sentence together! For instance, consider the example of Rule: Always start with a capital letter and end with a period. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Rule
examples.content: Always start with a capital letter and end with a period.

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Declarative: Spotting the Parts
slides.text: Let's look at: 'Matteo rides a calesa.' 'Matteo' is the subject because he is the person. 'rides a calesa' is the predicate (the action). Together, they tell us a complete factual statement. Notice the capital 'M' at the beginning of the sentence. Notice the period at the end of the sentence. Without a subject and predicate, a sentence is incomplete. Always check both parts when you write in your journal! To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Subject
examples.content: Matteo

examples.title: Predicate
examples.content: rides a calesa

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Story Time: The Calesa Ride
slides.text: Let's read a short story about Matteo's day: 'Matteo sits inside a wooden calesa on the street. The fast horse walks down the paved town road. Matteo smiles at the colorful houses along the way. He observes the busy community starting their morning.' This story is made of declarative sentences. They tell us exactly what Matteo does and sees. Read it aloud to practice your reading flow! For instance, consider the example of Reading Task: Read the story aloud to practice your spelling words! To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Reading Task
examples.content: Read the story aloud to practice your spelling words!

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Characters: Who is in the Story?
slides.text: Every story has characters that make it interesting. Characters are the people, animals, or creatures. They perform actions and speak words in the plot. In our story, the characters are Matteo and the horse. Understanding characters helps us connect with the story. We can think about how they feel and what they want. Who is your favorite character in the calesa story? Write down their names on your paper! For instance, consider the example of Key Concept: Characters = Who To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Key Concept
examples.content: Characters = Who

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Setting: Where and When?
slides.text: The setting is where and when the story takes place. It provides the background environment for the characters. In our story, the setting is a historic town street. The time of the story is a sunny morning. Knowing the setting helps us picture the events. It is like looking at a photograph of the scene. Where does your favorite daily activity take place? That place is the setting of your own daily story! For instance, consider the example of Key Concept: Setting = Where and When To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Key Concept
examples.content: Setting = Where and When

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Plot: What Happens?
slides.text: The plot is the sequence of events in a story. It tells us what happens first, next, and last. First, Matteo gets inside the wooden calesa. Next, the horse walks down the paved town street. Finally, Matteo smiles at the colorful houses. A clear plot makes stories easy to understand. It helps us follow the character's journey. Can you retell the plot of Matteo's ride in order? For instance, consider the example of Key Concept: Plot = What happens (First, Next, Then) To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Key Concept
examples.content: Plot = What happens (First, Next, Then)

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Observing Local Transport
slides.text: In our local community, we observe unique transport. A calesa is a carriage drawn by a horse on land. It has two large wheels and a wooden passenger cabin. A banca is a small outrigger boat used on water. It helps fishermen travel across rivers and seas. Both transport types are important to our heritage. They are perfect topics for our declarative sentences. Let's write a sentence about a banca today! To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Land Transport
examples.content: calesa

examples.title: Water Transport
examples.content: banca

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Nature Connections: The Wet Sand
slides.text: Let's walk down to the riverbank to explore. We observe wet sand near the flowing water. Let's write: 'This sand is wet and cold.' 'Sand' is a CVCC spelling word ending in 'nd'. 'This' points to the sand right under our feet. This sentence is a telling (declarative) sentence. It starts with a capital letter and ends with a period. We are using English to describe our science observations! For instance, consider the example of Observation: Pointing to sand near your feet: 'This is wet sand.' To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Observation
examples.content: Pointing to sand near your feet: 'This is wet sand.'

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Science Connection: The Pink Fish
slides.text: Look at the clear water in the stream. We point to a swimming fish: 'That is a pink fish.' 'Pink' is a CVCC word ending in the blend 'nk'. 'That' points to the fish swimming far from us. This is a factual observation about the natural world. It uses our senses to collect details about animals. Always look closely at the color of fish scales. What other scale colors can you observe in the water? For instance, consider the example of Observation: Pointing to fish in the pond: 'That is a pink fish.' To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Observation
examples.content: Pointing to fish in the pond: 'That is a pink fish.'

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Guided Practice: Spotting CVCC Words
slides.text: Let's play a game to spot CVCC words. Read this: 'The child sat at the wooden desk.' Which word ends with a double consonant blend? Yes, the word is 'desk'! It ends with 'sk'. The other words do not follow the CVCC pattern. Spelling 'desk' correctly requires the letters d-e-s-k. Can you find another CVCC word in your classroom? Look at the lamp or the window frame! For instance, consider the example of Check: d - e - s - k ends with the consonants s and k. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Check
examples.content: d - e - s - k ends with the consonants s and k.

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Guided Practice: Near or Far?
slides.text: Let's practice choosing the correct pronoun. If you are holding a flower, what do you say? 'A. This is a flower.' or 'B. That is a flower.'? Since the flower is in your hand, choose A! If a bird is high on a tree branch, you say: 'That is a bird on the branch.' Use 'this' for near, and 'that' for far away. It is as simple as measuring distance with your eyes! For instance, consider the example of Rule of Thumb: If you can touch it, use 'this' or 'these'. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Rule of Thumb
examples.content: If you can touch it, use 'this' or 'these'.

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Guided Practice: Capital and Period
slides.text: Let's check the rules for telling sentences. Look at: 'the banca floats in the river' What is missing from this declarative sentence? It must start with a capital 'T' for 'The'. It must end with a period at the end of 'river'. Let's write it correctly: 'The banca floats in the river.' Now it has a subject and a predicate. It is a complete and correct sentence! To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications.

examples.title: Corrected
examples.content: 'The banca floats in the river.'

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Common Error: Mixing Near and Far
slides.text: A common mistake is using 'this' for far objects. If a star is shining in the sky, do not say: 'This is a bright star.' (It is too far to touch!). Instead, say: 'That is a bright star.' If the books are far on the shelf, do not say: 'These are my books.' (They are out of reach!). Instead, say: 'Those are my books.' Always check the distance before you choose the pronoun! For instance, consider the example of Correction: 'Those clouds look soft.' To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Correction
examples.content: 'Those clouds look soft.'

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Summary of Week 1
slides.text: Congratulations! You completed all the slides for Week 1. Let's review the main language tools we mastered. We learned to decode CVCC words like tent, lamp, and desk. We learned to point using this, that, these, and those. We learned to write declarative sentences with periods. We learned to identify story characters, settings, and plots. We connected our English lessons to science observations. You are now ready to take the quiz and challenges! For instance, consider the example of Vocabulary: hand, tent, pink, lamp, bend, belt, milk, wind, desk, hunt To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.content: hand, tent, pink, lamp, bend, belt, milk, wind, desk, hunt

standard.q: Which word ends with a CVCC blend?
Identify the CVCC word in this sentence: 'The lamp is bright.'
Complete the word: 'The hikers slept in a green te__.'
Which of these words ends with the same blend as 'pink'?
Which CVCC word represents the blowing air we observe?
Complete the sentence for a pencil you are holding: '___ is my pencil.'
For two books on your lap, what do you say?
Complete: '___ calesa next to me is painted red.'
When pointing to your own two hands, you say:
Complete: '___ is my drawing of a pink flower in my lap.'
Complete for a bird flying high in the sky: '___ is a hawk.'
For many stars shining far away, we say:
Complete: '___ are bancas sailing in the distant river.'
Pointing to a mountain on the horizon, we say:
Complete: '___ is a horse drawing the calesa down the street yonder.'
What type of sentence is: 'Matteo smiles at the colorful houses.'
What is the subject of the sentence: 'The fast horse runs.'
What is the predicate of the sentence: 'Matteo rides a calesa.'
Which of these declarative sentences is punctuated correctly?
options: The banca sails on the river.
the banca sails on the river
The banca sails on the river?
the banca sails on the river.

standard.q: True or False: A telling (declarative) sentence always starts with a capital letter and ends with a period.
Who is the main character in the story of the calesa ride?
What is the setting of the calesa ride story?
options: A town street on a sunny day

standard.q: What happens first in the calesa ride story?
Which of these is a local transport we observed on water?
What does the word 'observing' mean in science and reading?
options: Using our senses to collect facts

challenge.q: Challenge: If you observe a bird flying far away and you write: 'this is a fast bird.' What two errors did you make?
options: Used 'this' instead of 'that', and did not capitalize the first letter.
Used 'that' instead of 'this', and forgot the period.
Used plural 'these' and forgot the subject.

challenge.q: Challenge: Which sentence has a CVCC word as the subject and another CVCC word in the predicate?
options: The wind blows the pink lamp.
The frog jumps on the rock.

challenge.q: Challenge: Read this passage: 'The wind blew the dry sand. A small crab ran to its hole.' What is the setting of this passage?
Challenge: Which of these is a declarative sentence that contains a scientific observation about a plant?
options: This green leaf has five points.
I love flowers because they are pretty.

challenge.q: Challenge: Unscramble the CVCC word in: 'We keep our books on a k-s-e-d.'

performance.title: My Nature Observation Journal
performance.desc: Observe three different natural objects around your home or garden (like a leaf, sand, or a lamp). Draw them on a piece of paper. For each drawing, write one declarative sentence using a demonstrative pronoun (This, That, These, Those) and at least one CVCC word. Read your sentences aloud to your parents.
labels: Correct use of CVCC words (e.g. hand, wind, desk, lamp, sand)
Correct use of Demonstrative Pronouns based on near/far observation
Proper declarative sentence structure (starts with capital, ends with period)

pages:
Exercise 1: CVCC Phonics Hunt

Write the correct CVCC word that matches each nature clue below using your stylus.

1. Blowing air that we feel but cannot see: wi__
wind
2. A tool that gives light when it is dark: la__
lamp
3. A shelter made of canvas used during outdoor camps: te__
tent

Exercise 2: Near and Far Pointing

Fill in the blank with the correct demonstrative pronoun (This, These, That, Those).

1. (Near) ___ is my hand observing the wet sand.
This
2. (Far) ___ are bancas sailing in the distant river.
Those
3. (Near) ___ are colorful flowers in my garden lap.
These

Exercise 3: Declarative Sentences & Story Elements

Write the subject or predicate for the story about Matteo's banca ride.

In 'Matteo sails a wooden banca.', write the Subject (who):
Matteo
Write the Predicate (what the subject does) in 'The wind blows the sails.':
blows the sails
